comments.author: @WhereonEarth4K
comments.text:
👇 Everything we curated for this Hawaii episode. Tours, books, cookbooks, travel resources.

*📚 BROWSE THE HAWAII COLLECTION*
  ◦ Every book, cookbook, and cultural item we'd recommend for this episode → https://amzn.to/4xoJGgn

*🛍 SEE OUR FULL W.O.E. STOREFRONT*
  ◦ Every destination Collection in one place → https://www.amazon.com/shop/whereonearth

*🎟 TOURS AND EXPERIENCES IN HAWAII*
  ◦ Volcano tours, snorkel trips, voyaging-canoe sails, and small-group island experiences → https://shorturl.at/dTuEP
  ◦ Browse all Hawaii experiences → https://gyg.me/DjDIrPxv

*📖 PLAN YOUR HAWAII TRIP*
  ◦ New Pocket Hawaiian Dictionary, Pukui and Elbert, the canonical reference → https://amzn.to/4voXCVF

*📚 GO DEEPER INTO HAWAII*
  ◦ Shoal of Time, Gavan Daws, the all-time bestselling history of the islands → https://amzn.to/4uUXRIo
  ◦ Hawaiki Rising, Sam Low, the definitive book on the Hōkūleʻa voyaging revival → https://amzn.to/49CPUPm

*🍲 BRING HAWAII HOME*
  ◦ Aloha Kitchen, Alana Kysar, IACP Award Finalist cookbook of the islands' multi-ethnic plate lunch tradition → https://amzn.to/43buDsw
  ◦ Clark Little: The Art of Waves, 150+ photographs from inside Hawaii's most powerful waves → https://amzn.to/4uMSTx0

How the fuck people survive here is ridiculous. I'm kanaka ma-ole born and raised and live on a military pension of less than $1200 a month . When I got back from Vietnam in
1968, I vowed. Never to support this fuckn country with taxes and worked under the table to support my family, I survive by common knowledge of my country. Aina and Kai always has supported 
My needs. Don't need this fucked system

comments.author: @Humanstatic1
comments.text: It is a blessing to call Honolulu home after previously living on Maui. Thank you for a film that was so beautiful and well done. The majority of visitors to Hawaii only come for a beach vacation and never see the real Hawaii. The Aina "land" is  one of Earth's greatest treasures.  I've harvested my own macadamia nuts, flown over lava crashing into the ocean and watched Nene cross the road.  Living here is a privilege that I never take for granted.  God bless you and yours.

comments.author: @Woodstock271
comments.text:
I lived on Oahu for 35 years. Surfing and fishing and exploring the jungles. Me and my dad sailed there on his Tahiti Ketch from San Francisco in 1987. Hawaii was supposed to be our first stop on a voyage around the world. Well, we got comfortable in Hawaii and stayed. Dad retired as captain of the Star of Honolulu (shown in this video off Waikiki), and he and his Canadian wife retired in Canada. I stayed another 10 years but it’s not the same Hawaii as it was when we arrived. Way too expensive and your pay never goes up. I moved to Seattle about 5 years ago. Better pay and lots more jobs. I work with 4 guys on the docks who are from Hawaii. They left for the same reason I did. But Washington is beautiful and not isolated like Hawaii. Yet, all we talk about is how much we miss home. And we’ll never go back. 
A strange mix of emotions about Hawaii. We love it but we can’t live there anymore. That isolation and no money to move everything overseas leaves you trapped. You have to abandon your material possessions and start over somewhere else, like most of us did. 
Excellent documentary! I know most the places you showed in the video at a glance. Aloha from Seattle 🤙🏼

replies.author: @maribelcolon9894
replies.text: Thank you for sharing ❤

replies.author: @Woodstock271
replies.text: @kimcamper6655, It’s the same on any boat. You piss over the rail when the weather allows it. Otherwise, you pee in a one gallon water jug and then pour it over the side later. For a woman, they can’t aim their urine like men. So they get a bucket to squat over. No problem, it all works out. At night in a storm out at sea and you have to take a crap, we use the toilet in the head it’s fine to open the drain valve when you’re 5 miles from shore. In a harbor you’re not allowed to open the sewage holding tank at all, ever. Nobody wants shit floating around the harbor. Like everything on a boat, space is limited. If you want to empty your holding tank, you just cast off and go for a day sail. Out 5 miles it’s legal to open the drain. Now on a fishing boat with no head (bathroom) it’s a 5 gallon bucket for going number two. With water in it. You can’t crap in a dry bucket. Has to have water in it and the other fishermen look away, and we’re 25 five miles out and you pour your spoils over the rail. I’ve seen rookies on board asking where they can crap and we point to the bucket and they don’t know they have to put seawater in it first and they crap in a dry bucket, and not to be too graphic, it’s a mess they have to clean out of the bucket. With seawater in it, it pours over the side like flushing a toilet. Pretty clean. We may need this bucket for something else like bait and whatever, so every bucket is cleaned afterwards. Sometimes it’s an emergency and you pick the closest bucket and do your thing and now you have to clean it with ocean water. Sailors and fishermen are pretty crude but we feed the families on land and nobody sees how we deal with biological realities.

replies.author: @Woodstock271
replies.text:
@WhereonEarth4K, Mahalo. You really captured the incredible beauty and lifestyle in Hawaii and then also the modern truth about the sad mass exodus from Hawaii. Unaffordable to live there now. It was a pretty easy life when I got there in 87’. One full-time job payed the rent and bills and the rest was playtime. Surfing, fishing, sailing, and partying with friends. There were zero homeless people back then in Hawaii, and there weren’t any either, on the mainland when I left. I don’t remember what year it was when my rent doubled and then tripled and the prices of everything skyrocketed but about the early 2000’s. I scrambled and got a Union job in construction so I was still okay, but then the Union-Busters started taking the unions away (the republicans of course), and Democratic Hawaii didn’t fight hard enough or they were paid off. Either way, the average became the poor and the poor became the homeless. My friends from Hawaii left before I did, I stubbornly tried to stick it out but it just got worse and worse. To ship my truck I bought brand new in 97’ would cost $10,000 to the mainland. The blue book listed it at $3000. I just gave my truck to an old Hawaiian friend and gave away everything to friends and left. Same story with the other Hawaiians I met in Washington. So sad. 
Excellent video! Keep up the great work! 🤙🏼

replies.author: @Tequawitha
replies.text: @Woodstock271 They do not have the worst education… YOU ARE VERY WRONG ABOUT EVERYTHING. My child received a scholarship to a Japanese University… she is now a teacher in Japan. I have two nurses and a teacher as children. You have no clue what you are talking about. Happy you went back to the Mainland. We don’t need people like cutting down Hawai’i. A girl from CA… called my daughter the “N” word. She never heard that word before. Mainland children try make gangs… did not work on Maui.

replies.author: @Woodstock271
replies.text:
@deborahcabanting, Way off base. And no, Hawaii has the worst educational system of all 50 states. If you’re educated there, you cannot compete with the mainland. The biggest problem in Hawaii is the corrupt government. Allowing foreign investors from Japan and China to buy up all the land and jack up prices that nobody can afford. Anyone who lives near any of the countless military bases sees their rents double because of government subsidized military housing. The corrupt police syndicate runs the drug syndicate in Hawaii and meth is rampant on all the islands. Hawaii has sold itself out to the highest bidder, just like the rest of the country has, under a wildly corrupt government. It’s not the cause of people like me who arrived with nothing, worked 35 years there and left with nothing. The problem has always been the inept government. On Kauai and Maui, they’ve allowed millionair celebrities to buy up beachfront property in Poipu and Koloa, driving up real estate prices beyond affordable for average working class. This video explains how families who lived in Hawaii for generations are leaving for the mainland and better opportunities, better education, lower cost of living. 
If you’re doing fine on Maui, that’s great. Maui no ka oi. 🤙🏼

replies.author: @Tequawitha
replies.text: Live on Maui…..we are fine. Never have been without a job. My children are well educated. Too many people take advantage of the islands that are not from here. Monopolizing our islands that raises the prices our prices and demand. Visit and go home instead of staying and ruining the islands. 💔😢💔😢💔😢💔😢💔😢

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thirty-five years, a ketch from San Francisco, four dock workers who all left for the same reason — that's the Hawaii this film was reaching for underneath the postcard. The "trapped by what you love" thread is one of the most honest things about those islands. Thank you for writing that out. 🌿

comments.author: @beachgirl9234
comments.text:
Birds seem to be going more extinct in Guam where the snakes are out of control, killing the birds. 
Hawaii is Heaven on earth.
The traditions and people need to be cherished. 
God bless Hawaiian Islands and the beautiful people of these stunning Island's.

replies.author: @beachgirl9234
replies.text: ​@WhereonEarth4Kyour film is beautiful and informative. I enjoy reading what other people are writing as well,  especially the story about the fellow and his father, who ended up living in Hawaii for many years. The reason they moved is quite sad to me but true facts for so many. I also currently live where many people from Hawaii have moved to because it is so difficult to be able to afford to live there. I lived in Guam for 4 years, and traveled through Hawaii to get back to the states on occasion. Even in the mid to latter 1980s when I lived in guam, there were several power outages because of the brown snake. I'm so glad Hawaii has mongoose, and that does not happen there for that reason. I was amazed to learn about the other reasons they are losing their bird population. The taste of pineapple and coconut in avocado and so many other foods on the islands are so fresh and so amazingly flavorful that, sometimes I forgot the rest of the foods are shipped in. Many blessings to all who remain living there.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K

comments.author: @katja8321
comments.text: I'm living next to the Village where Joseph Deveuster lived in Belgium.  We have a museum with his statue and he's still celebrated every year

replies.author: @katja8321
replies.text:
​@WhereonEarth4K we had school themes about it every year. We used to sell pens for the "damiaan actie" during that week. 

In the local church they did crafts to sell to help a project in moloka'i untill 20 years ago. We also had nuns that went to moloka'i.

We have a local school , elderly center, street, etc named after him.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That connection between a small Belgian village and a remote Hawaiian peninsula is one of the most remarkable threads in the whole story. What's it like growing up so close to that legacy?

comments.author: @HestoryHimstoryHistory
comments.text: Where did the People come from

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That question is one of the most astonishing in the whole story — Polynesian navigators reached Hawaii from thousands of miles away using stars, ocean currents, and birds, with no instruments. The film touches it, but the full answer is even more remarkable. 🌿
